paragraphs.text: After Jacob and Laban had parted, “the angels of God met Jacob” (Gen 32:1). By this they gave Jacob to understand that if Laban had not obeyed God, who had appeared to him in the evening, then on the next morning he and all those with him would have been put to death by the hand of the angels who guard Jacob. For as at his coming the angels accompanying him had been shown to him, so at his return God shows him angels, assuring him of what He had said: “I will go with thee, and I will bring thee thence.” The host of angels was shown to Jacob also so that he might not fear Esau, because those who accompanied him were more numerous than those who came with Esau. Then Jacob “sent… messengers… to Esau his brother” — excusing himself for having tarried. And when Jacob heard that Esau was “coming to meet him” with four hundred men (Gen 32:3–4, 6), he had recourse with prayer to God, asking Him to remember the covenant made with him at his going forth; but also to his brother he sent appeasing gifts, that he might not bear in mind the offense done to him on the day when Jacob had stolen from him the blessing.

On that same night an angel appeared to Jacob “and wrestled with him” (Gen 32:24). Jacob overcame the angel, but was also overcome by the angel, that he might learn through this how weak he was and how strong. He was weak in that, when the angel “touched… his thigh” (Gen 32:25), it went out of joint. And he was strong, in that the angel said to him: “Let me go.” And showing Jacob how long they had wrestled, the angel added: “for the day breaketh” (Gen 32:26). And Jacob asked him for a blessing, because they had wrestled with each other in love. And the angel blessed Jacob, showing by this that he was not angry with one who had withstood him, being a man of dust. So God fulfilled all that He had promised to Jacob. For He enriched him, brought him out and went with him, as He had promised him; delivered him from Laban and saved him from his brother Esau. However Jacob, who had vowed to God at his going forth to pay a tithe, gathered it together, but in fear sent it to Esau. Therefore his thigh too was changed, even as he himself had changed his word. And he who “strove” (Gen 32:28) with the angel, who is fire, now stands before Esau lame, though he feels no pain.
